data.attributionText: from The American Heritage® Dictionary of the English Language, 5th Edition.

data.text: A room or building equipped for scientific experimentation or research.
data.attributionText: from The American Heritage® Dictionary of the English Language, 5th Edition.

data.text: An academic period devoted to work or study in such a place.
data.attributionText: from The American Heritage® Dictionary of the English Language, 5th Edition.

data.text: A place where drugs and chemicals are manufactured.
data.attributionText: from The American Heritage® Dictionary of the English Language, 5th Edition.

data.text: A place for practice, observation, or testing.
data.attributionText: from The American Heritage® Dictionary of the English Language, 5th Edition.

data.text: A room, building, or workshop especially fitted with suitable apparatus for conducting investigations in any department of science or art, or for elaborating or manufacturing chemical, medicinal, or any similar products: as, a chemical or pharmaceutical laboratory; hence, also, figuratively, any place where or in which similar processes are carried on by natural forces.
Milit., an establishment for the manufacture of rockets, port-fires, fuses, percussion-caps, quick-and slow-matches, friction-primers, electric primers, etc., designed for military operations.
The space between the fire and the flue-bridges of a reverberatory furnace in which the work is performed.
The workroom of a chemist; also, a place devoted to experiments in any branch of natural science. Hence, by extension, a place where something is prepared, or some operation is performed.
data.attributionText: from the GNU version of the Collaborative International Dictionary of English.
examples: the liver is the <ex>laboratory</ex> of the bile

data.text: Any place, activity or situation suggestive of a scientific laboratory{1}, especially in being conducive to learning new facts by experimentation or by systematic observation.
data.attributionText: from the GNU version of the Collaborative International Dictionary of English.
examples: the states serve as <ex>laboratories</ex> where different new policies may be tested prior to adoption throughout the country
